topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thin, the paper cheap, and the handwriting was a looping script I vaguely recognized from distant family photos. Inside, a name: Elias Vance. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I hadn't expected this. I certainly hadn't expected *him*. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I stared at the name, then at the faded photograph tucked beneath it—a smiling man whose eyes were eerily familiar. A sharp, hot anger began to bubble up, quickly morphing into a heavy, suffocating weight. Why now? Why this secret dropped onto my doorstep, years after I’d built this life brick by painstaking brick? I crumpled the paper, the sound loud in the quiet apartment. I needed distance. I needed to erase this intrusion before it contaminated everything I thought I knew about myself.

[story 2]

"Another one?" I asked, my voice clipped, the sound grating even to my own ears. I didn't look up from the ledger spread across the kitchen table. My jaw was tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. This was just another obligation, another unexpected demand placed upon my already overflowing schedule. The mention of a half-sibling, a ghost from a branch of the family tree I never bothered to trace, felt like an unwelcome tax. I pushed the papers away with unnecessary force, the crisp edges catching my fingertips. A low growl vibrated in my chest. It was unfair. Unjust. All these years of careful construction, of silencing the noise, and now this. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, the floorboards groaning under my sudden, agitated movement. I needed to burn this correspondence, to make the connection vanish, but my hands were shaking too badly to manage the task cleanly.

[story 3]

I reread the note three times, the ink blurring slightly under my intense scrutiny. The sheer audacity of it—that someone, somewhere, had kept this piece of my existence hidden, just waiting for the opportune moment to reveal it. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I felt a distinct tightening around my eyes, a sudden, unwelcome prickling behind them. It wasn't sadness; it was pure, undiluted irritation. This discovery felt like an unwelcome intrusion into a perfectly ordered universe I had meticulously maintained. I tossed the letter onto the desk. It landed with a dull thud. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, my posture radiating a deep sense of being cornered and profoundly annoyed. I didn't want to know. I certainly didn't want this complication.
